verses.commentary: Theophany at Mamre. The LORD appears directly. Heat of the day suggests midday rest. Abraham is at his tent entrance, hospitable posture.
Three men appear. One is the LORD (verse 1), the other two are angels (19:1). Abraham doesn't initially recognize them as divine. He hurries and bows, showing eastern hospitality. Urgent welcome.
Abraham's humility. My lord could be polite address or recognition of status. Do not pass by is an earnest request. Hospitality is a sacred duty.
Water for washing feet. Standard hospitality in a dusty climate. Rest under the tree offers shade and comfort.
Abraham offers food. Refreshed for the journey. Now that you have come acknowledges the honor of their presence.
Abraham hurries again. Three seahs is about 20 quarts, a large amount. Finest flour shows generosity. He involves Sarah in the hospitality.
Running, selecting choice meat, hurrying. Exceptional hospitality. A calf is a feast, not a snack.
Complete meal. Curds, milk, meat. Abraham stands while they eat, a servant's posture. He doesn't presume to sit with them. Hebrews 13:2 notes some have entertained angels unknowingly.
The question is specific. They know her name. This signals divine knowledge. Sarah is listening from the tent.
The promise repeated, now to Sarah. About this time next year matches 17:21. Sarah hears directly. The tent entrance allows eavesdropping.
Reality check. Very old. Past the age means the way of women had ceased. Post-menopausal. Biologically impossible.
Sarah laughs inwardly. Worn out means used up. My lord refers to Abraham respectfully. Will I have pleasure? suggests both the pleasure of a child and perhaps sexual pleasure, given their age.
The LORD knows her inner laughter. He confronts it directly. Divine omniscience.
The key question. Too hard means too wonderful, too difficult. Nothing is impossible for God. The promise is reaffirmed with confidence.
Sarah denies it out of fear. She lied. The LORD corrects her. Yes, you did. God sees the heart and confronts falsehood even from the matriarch.
The men rise. They look toward Sodom, foreshadowing judgment. Abraham escorts them, continuing hospitality and perhaps sensing something significant.
God deliberates. Shall I hide? implies Abraham has a right to know. Remarkable intimacy. God shares his plans with his friend (James 2:23).
Reason for disclosure. Abraham's role in blessing all nations means he must understand justice and judgment. His descendants will need to know God's character.

I have chosen him (literally I have known him) suggests intimate selection. Direct his children means teach, command. The way of the LORD is righteousness and justice. Teaching future generations ensures the promise continues.
Outcry suggests oppressed victims crying out. So great and so grievous. The language is strong. Sodom's sin is not just individual immorality but systemic injustice.
Go down echoes Babel (11:5). God investigates before judging. Divine justice is thorough and fair. I will know shows God doesn't act on hearsay but on evidence.
The two angels leave. Abraham stays with the LORD. Remained standing suggests he lingers intentionally. The intercession begins.
Abraham's boldness. Approached suggests drawing near with purpose. Will you sweep away? challenges God's justice. This is audacious prayer.
First offer. Fifty righteous. Will you spare the place for their sake? Abraham argues for mercy based on the righteous minority.
Abraham's theology. Far be it means profane, unthinkable. Judge of all the earth must do right, must do justice. Abraham holds God to God's own character.
God agrees. For their sake. Fifty righteous would save the city. Mercy for the many based on the few.
Abraham's humility. Been so bold acknowledges the audacity. Dust and ashes recalls human mortality and insignificance (Job 42:6). Yet he continues.
Abraham bargains down. Forty-five. God agrees again. The negotiation continues.
Forty. God's patience continues. The Lord participates in this remarkable dialogue.
Abraham worries about divine anger but presses on. Thirty. God remains merciful.
Twenty. God's mercy extends further than Abraham initially imagined.

Final offer. Ten. God agrees. Why does Abraham stop at ten? Perhaps the minimum for a minyan (quorum for prayer in Judaism). Perhaps exhaustion or awareness that Lot's household doesn't reach ten. The intercession ends.
The encounter ends. The LORD leaves. Abraham returns home. The intercession reveals God's mercy and Abraham's growing boldness in prayer. Tragically, not even ten righteous are found.
